explanations.5: DOCKER_OS_GROUPID is set to 1000, which is a default numeric identifier for user groups in Linux—this will be used later to create the actual group.
explanations.6: DOCKER_OS_USERID is set to 1000, which is a default numeric identifier for users in Linux—this will be used later to create the actual user account.
explanations.19: The 'set -e -x' command makes the shell exit immediately if any command fails, and print each command before running it—this helps catch errors early in Docker builds.
explanations.21: The addgroup command creates a new group with the ID stored in DOCKER_OS_GROUPID and names it after the username variable.
explanations.23: The flags -S and -D make this a system user (not a regular login user), -G assigns it to the group created on the previous line, and -h sets its home directory.
explanations.25: The chown command changes ownership of the home folder so that the newly created user (not root) owns all their own files.
explanations.29: These ARG lines redefine the same build arguments again—this resets them for a new build stage, since each FROM statement starts fresh in Docker.
explanations.39: These ARG lines reference the build arguments again for the second stage; they're needed because each FROM creates a new layer that doesn't inherit ARG values automatically.
